item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked the premise, but the additional stuff lost it for me.

item.title: Ohai
item.content:encoded:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=dnFsYxzqtFU

Could not stop thinking about this. May not have been intentional, but I laughed so much. Thanks. Needed that today.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Pretty much exactly what he said. It's well written but rubs me the wrong way. Particularly the bit about the O5 council. For the Foundation to function, they have to be surrounded by a manifold layer of precautions and protections. Not the sort of group that randomly goes "Oops! Contaminated!" Heck, I think folks used to say they were picked from people who had never directly handled SCPs for exactly this reason.

Gotta downvote, sorry man. If I'm too thick to get some subtle, masterful implication, someone by all means let me know.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked it! not what i would call an incredible SCP that gives chills, but good enough to deserve an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't know if i like it. On one hand, it's interesting. On the other, it's an unstoppable keter, anyone can aproach you, tell his name and you become an infected instance. It's too much

So, neutral vote

item.title: Very good thread
item.content:encoded: One of the best I have ever read. In my opinion, one of the best from the SCP site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it a lot. It's one of those fun articles that plays with a unsustainably complex wad of paradox - containing a memetic infohazard. The Foundation is just damn lucky this is such a benign one.
Also, I am very amused by the notion of Bright getting infected.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm new, and I don't have any criticisms that haven't already been addressed, just felt like stopping in to say thanks for a good read; I was definitely feeling uneasy by the last bit.

This is one of my favorites so far.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's a very interesting effect, constructed pretty well. The hook at the end is pretty neat, but not my style. I'm neutral voting this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So, the current documentation is actually partially false and we will never learn the truth behind the scp?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you for giving critique on the article, Esko.

EchoFourDelta pointed out something like this earlier, saying the entire article didn't make sense (resulting in me going back to edit it some more). I'm sorry if some of the details didn't come across as clear to you.

The very first line in the containment procedures is one I've used verbatim on another SCP. That line was also one I took from an older SCP. Both cases of it being used has worked well to my knowledge.

In short, the name (if you were to research it in-universe), results in you learning about a Caucasian man (which is common, but not always true). Learning the name makes you think you are the man. The disputes makes them "overwrite" the original information they've received when they first became instances, essentially creating a new identity for them.

"Safe information" refers to information that has been confirmed to not cause SCP-2150 infection. The O5 are able to receive said info without coming into contact with the anomaly. Whatever "info" they receive is done so indirectly. The "Original Documentation" is the first written document of 2150.

Also, I was careful of making sure there was no direct implication of the O5 being infected. The last letter states that they have no knowledge of the name (except for the retired O5 member).

Everything else you have pointed out has been fixed accordingly. Thank you once again. Your input helps me to become a better writer.

If anything else comes off an unclear, feel free to ask.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Some very, very weird phrasing throughout. I ordinarily hate nitpicking, but there's a point where the nits are bigger than the rest of the piece.

Nitpicking
Hide

Information related to SCP-2150 is to be blocked from all public service providers.

The woman at Subway who makes me breakfast is a person who provides a public service. Do you mean ISP instead?

the most common resembling a Caucasian male with facial hair.

Me, Burt Reynolds, George R.R. Martin, and Marc Maron are all Caucasian men with facial hair. It's just not a "resemblance" per se. You can say that all or most of the affected by the memetic anomaly are Caucasian men with facial hair, though that begs the question of "why not get elderly female Asian D-class personnel to just do all of the clerical work for this one?"

despite similar search results being yielded between personnel.

How do you yield something between something?

Instances of SCP-2150-1 express confusion, stating that they have no memories, except for information related to SCP-2150.

What are they confused about, then? They're deluded, certainly, but not confused. They sound pretty sure of themselves to me.

Over time, multiple disputes among instances will severely alter any original information gained from themselves.

…what? Information gained from themselves? Like, I imagine you have an image in your head of what these Theta-10 events look like, but the image is not conveying itself into my head.

Reintroduction of information to SCP-2150 will overwrite any previous information given from other instances of SCP-2150-1.

SCP-2150 is the memetic phenomenon associated with the name; how do you introduce information into it? Again, I'm sure this makes sense to you, but I'm not picking up on it.

D-8327M and other infected personnel were detained and administered amnestics before being issued back to Site-███.

The Researcher was detained and is currently being issued to Site-██.

Issued? You mean, returned?

Revealing more information to you would come so at the risk of infection.

Would come so at? You can just say "would risk infection".

As I am well aware, some O5 members have undergone amnestic treatment prior to promotion, and I am no different.

How could you say a thing without having been well aware of it? The sentence basically comes off as exposition for the audience's benefit…which is fine in general, as long as it doesn't look like that.

During the writing of SCP-2150, I have received safe information regarding the former Site Director. I have reason to suspect that prior to my promotion, I was previously regarded as this Site Director. My current biological ancestry and criminal records are identical to this man.

Okay, first of all, what the hell even is "safe information" in this case? How does anybody believe anything said about this SCP to be "safe"? For that matter, why is all of this stuff restricted below Level 4 or 5, but the most important and powerful people in the Foundation have free access to uncontained memetic and infohazardous materials?

Also, "prior to… I was previously regarded as this Site Director" is just "I was the Site Director." All things "prior" are past tense; all things associated with "was" are past tense; all things that happened "previously" are past tense. You don't need three different words to remind the reader that a thing happened in the not-now-or-in-the-future-time. You can keep the "prior to my promotion" part, but you don't need "was previously" on top of that. "I was the Site Director prior to my promotion" does the same job more economically.

If this is true, then I am considered a threat not only to the O5 Council, but also to the Foundation as well.

No, he is a threat. Nobody considers him a threat until they read this and find out that he's a threat. Again, economy of language.

Therefore, I request that I am to be resigned from the O5 Council.

"Resign" can be a reflexive verb in some instances (i.e. "I have resigned myself to perdition"), but not a transitive one (i.e. "I resign you to perdition"). He can resign, an act which the O5 Council can then accept or reject implicitly, but that's it. You use the same phrasing again in the next letter.

We deny a request to view documentation, due to risk of exposure.

The sentiment is intelligent, but the request is again malformed. "We will not review the information" can do it, but if the O5s are "denying a request to view documentation", that usually means they are denying somebody else's request that somebody else be allowed to review documentation.

We will keep the original documentation to SCP-2150, so that proper counter measures are enforced when needed be.

Which original documentation? We've referred to something like five different iterations of the documentation. I don't even know which version I read at the beginning of the document. Also, "countermeasures", "can be enforced" (sort of a future subjunctive mood), and either "when needed" or "when they need to be".

Looking further into his criminal records indicate that the man was convicted countless charges.

Looking…indicates, convicted of, and there is a literal count to the charges that can be ennumerated; your phrasing is just a sort of silly way of making the guy sound super duper bad. You can have six million counts of jaywalking on your record and I'm not gonna be terribly worried about you.

It is not certain whether this is true because of the nature of SCP-2150 or the name associated with it. The original documentation of SCP-2150 is now missing; it is likely that the former O5-██ has taken the document with him.

This actually highlights how silly the whole first exchange there was. "I have good reason to suspect that I've secretly been an anomalous pseudo-sleeper agent embedded in the most powerful circle of one of the most powerful groups on the earth. So…I guess I'm just gonna pack my stuff and head back to the house, right?" "Oh, no, we're vaguely sure that you're not an anomalous pseudo-sleeper agent embedded in the most powerful circle of one of the most powerful groups on the earth, you're just somewhat insane. So yes, you can pack your stuff and head back to the house." How was this person allowed to just clean out their desk and go home?

It is now the Foundation's interest to track down this man, in which some sources refer to him as "The Worst Man in The World".

It is now in the Foundation's interests to track down this man, in which some sources refer to him as "The Worst Man in the World. Additionally, that's a really, really weird title for a group of adults to use. It's not "Most Wanted" or "Top Priority;" it's like the name a group of kids who are the Good Guys use to refer to the top Bad Guy on the playground.

It was never intended to have SCP-2150 thrown at us.

That…is a weird thing to even have to say. To reinforce that the O5 Council dislikes having SCPs used against them. As if someone thought that they were okay with it.

All we know for certain is that hazardous information was presented to us after the promotion of O5-██. Harsher containment procedures on infohazards are the only way we can prevent a situation like this from happening again.

Then every member of the O5 Council is potentially contaminated and needs to be relieved of duty and detained for experimentation. That's the only reasonable conclusion to take from that sentence. Finally, containment procedures need to be more effective and more consistently carried out, not "harsher."

In general, it comes off an SCP tailor-made to make the Foundation look sort of dumb, and the actions of the O5 Council don't help to give off the impression that any of them were ever qualified to run an Exxon station, let alone the Foundation. Downvoted for this conceptual error and the many weird phrasing situations happening throughout.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…

We've got a lot of D-Class who're completely innocent, don't we? And this is why, isn't it?

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interesting, but "a Caucasian male with facial hair" sounds a bit too informal and generic to me. Doesn't prevent an upvote though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hmm, I like it too. At first it looked like people forming a religion on what constitutes the one true mark. Then it got deeper.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hey guys. This is my first Keter, and I wrote this in the span of 3 and a half days. It's a double challenge for myself, so I hope its received well.

If you haven't already, make sure to check out this tale based on SCP-2150.

I'd like to thank Soulless, djkaktus, Zolgamax, Weizhong, Dr_Leonard, Azzleflux, DJRaven, Echo, and EdAWACS for looking over my draft in chat and helping me. Holy crap that's a lot of people.

I would especially like to thank Echo for calling me out on how ridiculous this skip sounded prior.

Note: There's a lot more than what appears on the surface.
